questions.q: In a trie (prefix tree), what is the time complexity of searching for a string of length m in a trie with n strings?
options: O(m) regardless of n
O(m log n)
O(n)
O(m × n)
questions.explanation: Trie search is O(m) where m is the string length, independent of how many strings are stored (n). Each character requires following one edge, and there are at most m characters. Hash tables also achieve O(m) for search, but tries allow prefix-based operations.

questions.q: What is the space complexity of a trie storing n strings with alphabet size σ?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
O(n × σ) worst case for total characters
O(σ^n)
questions.explanation: A trie uses O(total characters) space in the worst case, which is at most O(n × σ) where σ is the alphabet size (26 for English letters). Each node can have up to σ children. This is more space-efficient than hash tables when strings share prefixes.

questions.q: In an autocomplete system, which operation does a trie excel at that a hash table cannot efficiently do?
options: Finding all strings with a given prefix
Checking if a string exists
Counting total strings
Deleting a string
questions.explanation: Tries naturally support prefix queries by traversing to a node and collecting all descendants, which is O(prefix length + output size). Hash tables must check every string, requiring O(n) time. This makes tries ideal for autocomplete and IP routing.

questions.q: What is a compressed trie (or radix tree)?
options: A trie where nodes with only one child are merged into single edges labeled with strings
A trie encoded using Huffman compression
A trie that only stores vowels and consonants separately
A trie with a maximum depth limit
questions.explanation: A compressed trie merges chains of single-child nodes into edges labeled with substrings, reducing space to O(n) rather than O(n × σ) for n distinct strings. This improves space efficiency while maintaining O(m) search time.

questions.q: In a trie, to mark 'cat' as a complete word distinct from 'catch', what should you do?
options: Delete the node for 't' in 'cat'
Mark the node representing 'cat' with an end-of-word flag
Create a separate trie for complete words
Store a pointer to the root in the 't' node
questions.explanation: Each trie node needs a boolean flag indicating if it represents the end of a valid word. This allows 'cat' and 'catch' to coexist: both path paths exist, but only their respective nodes have the flag set.

questions.q: How do trie insertions compare to hash table insertions in worst-case time complexity?
options: Trie insertion is O(1), hash table is O(m)
Both are O(m) where m is string length or hash computation time
Hash table is always faster
Trie is always faster
questions.explanation: Trie insertion is O(m) to traverse m characters. Hash table insertion is also O(m) on average due to hashing the entire string. The difference is that trie insertion order doesn't affect structure (deterministic), while hash tables require a good hash function.

questions.q: What is the main disadvantage of tries compared to hash tables for exact string matching?
options: Tries are slower for exact lookups
Tries use more memory than hash tables for non-overlapping strings
Tries cannot handle Unicode characters
Tries require rehashing when full
questions.explanation: If strings share no common prefixes, tries waste space creating separate branches for each character. Hash tables hash directly to a bucket with O(1) average case. Tries excel when many strings share prefixes (common in natural language).

questions.q: In a trie with strings 'car', 'card', 'care', 'careful', how many nodes are needed (counting root)?
options: 6 nodes
11 nodes
12 nodes
15 nodes
questions.explanation: Root, then c(1), a(2), r(3), d(4-end), e(5), c(6), a(7), r(8), e(9), f(10), u(11), l(12-end). The branching happens after 'car': one branch to 'd', one to 'e'. Total including root: 12 nodes.

questions.q: For IP routing tables, why are tries (or radix trees) preferred over hash tables?
options: Tries are faster than hash tables
Tries support longest-prefix matching, finding the most specific route for a given IP prefix
Hash tables cannot store IP addresses
Tries use less memory than any alternative
questions.explanation: IP routing requires longest-prefix matching to find the most specific (longest matching) prefix for a destination IP. Tries naturally support this by traversing the tree. Hash tables cannot efficiently solve this because they match exact keys, not prefixes.

questions.q: What time complexity improvement does a ternary search tree (TST) offer over a standard trie with a large alphabet?
options: O(m) to O(log m) for search
O(m) to O(m log σ) where σ is alphabet size, with better cache locality and space savings
No improvement in time complexity, only space
O(m) to O(1) amortized
questions.explanation: A TST uses three child pointers (left, equal, right) instead of σ children, reducing space from O(σ) per node to O(1) and improving cache performance. Search is O(m log σ) in practice, but worst-case is still O(m). Useful when σ is large.
